sections.paragraph:
not used in Kal, pr. to be smooth [“to make smooth”], hence to slip away, to escape, i.q. the kindred פָּלַט. (Of the same family are the roots cited under מָלַח No. I. The third radical is a dental or a sibilant in מָלַץ, Arabic ملد II. to soften, ملث to smooth, to flatter, ملس to be soft, smooth, ملز to escape, to slip away, Gr. μέλδω to soften, μαλάσσω, μειλίσσω, although in these the root has γ, [comp. ἀμέλγω, μαλακός, mulgeo, mulceo]; Germ. mild, etc. With the letter l hardened is formed מָרַט, which see.)

Piel מִלֵּט and מִלַּט-

(1) to cause to escape, to deliver from danger, Job 6:23 29:12 . מִלַּט נַפְשֹׁו to preserve one’s own life, 1Ki 1:12 מִלַּט נֶפֶשׁ פּ׳ to preserve any one’s life, 2Sa 19:6 Psa 116:4.

(2) to lay eggs, pr. to cause to escape, Isa 34:15 see Hiph. No. 2. (Arab. مَلِيطُ fœtus.)

Hiphil-

(1) to deliver, to rescue, Isa 31:5.

(2) to bring forth, Isa 66:7.

Niphal-

(1) to be delivered, Psa 22:6 more often however reflect. to deliver oneself, to escape, Gen 19:19 1Sa 30:17 Pro 11:21 19:5 28:26 .

(2) to go away in haste without the notion of flight, 1Sa 20:29.

Hithpael, i.q. Niph. Job 19:20, נָאֶתְמַלְּטָה בְּעֹור שִׁנָּי “I have (hardly) escaped with the skin of my teeth,” proverb. for, there hardly remains anything sound in my body. (Arab. by a similar proverb زجا براسه to go away with one’s head, i.e. to preserve one’s life.) Poet. used of sparks flying about, Job 41:11. Hence-
